summary: Gruber announces that he is available for hire as a freelance web developer, designer, and technical writer. After two years of publishing Daring Fireball, he acknowledges that while the site's revenue from T-shirts, memberships, and sponsorships exceeds typical blog income, it remains firmly short of a full-time salary. He admits that self-promotion doesn't come naturally to him but recognizes the practical necessity. The post lists his skills — technical writing, web standards development, Perl/PHP coding, and Mac expertise — and opens the door to both contract work and full-time employment.
key_points: Daring Fireball's revenue has grown beyond a typical blog but remains insufficient as a primary income source
Gruber frames his site as a 'very lucrative hobby' still in the 'don't quit your day job' category
He overcomes his natural aversion to self-promotion out of practical financial necessity
His skill set spans technical writing, web standards development, Perl/PHP, and Mac expertise
The post marks a pivotal moment where Gruber publicly seeks to turn his reputation into sustainable work
money_quotes: The revenue from this web site is well beyond that of a typical weblog — but, it's way short of a full-time salary.
It's turned into a very lucrative hobby, but the numbers are still firmly in the "don't quit your day-job" category.
Self-promotion does not come naturally to me. I'd much prefer to let my work speak for itself.
Unfortunately, you can't pay rent with a well-written weblog, or with consulting services no one is aware of.
I am currently free, where by "free", I mean under-employed and available.
I am a technical writer who can present and explain complex subject matter engagingly.
key_insight: Even with a successful and influential blog, Gruber found that reputation alone couldn't pay the bills — a remarkably honest admission from someone who would later prove that independent publishing could become a full career.
